front: Practice: this booth's roof, called s'chach, must be made of plant matter cut from the ground (bamboo, wood, or pine/palm branches, never straw), symbolizing God's protective "clouds of glory"
Practice: according to Rabbi Yochanan, God will build one for the righteous in the world to come from the shining skin of the Leviathan

Practice: 2010 design competitions, including one in New York's Union Square Park ("Sukkah City"), showcased experimental versions of this booth, with entries in one case required to be built atop a camel
Practice: the Mishnah's tractate on this booth (sixth in the order of Mo'ed) permits a two-poster but not a four-poster bed inside, and forbids building it atop a pre-existing colonnade
Practice: Karaites hold that the aravot nahal (willow branches) should form part of this booth's structure rather than be used inside it; Rabbi Yose says it may be built either bottom-up or top-down
Practice: in Israeli apartment buildings, only the topmost of two stacked versions of this booth is considered valid, leading to offset or terraced balconies
Practice: a prayer recited inside this booth includes the phrase "tzivanu leishev ba-[this]" ("commanded us to dwell in the sukkah")
Practice: consists of the lulav (palm), etrog (citron), hadass (myrtle), and aravah (willow), collectively the Four Species/Four Kinds, waved or shaken during Sukkot's prayers
Practice: according to the Mishnah, an etrog is not kosher for use if it is completely round or missing its "nipple" (pitam)
Practice: two of these four plants, the aravah and hadass, are said to resemble the mouth and eyes respectively
Practice: seven "exalted guests" — traditionally Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, Aaron, Joseph, and David, the "seven shepherds" of Israel — are ritually invited into the sukkah, one per day of Sukkot
Practice: many Jews decorate the sukkah with a diagram depicting these seven symbolic guests
Practice: some communities instead or additionally invoke seven female guests, the ushpizot, over the seven days of Sukkot
Practice: the five (or six, outside Israel) intermediate days of Sukkot are called Chol HaMoed, during which the Ya'aleh v'Yavo prayer is recited
Practice: on the final day of Sukkot, Hoshana Rabbah, worshippers circle the synagogue seven times carrying the lulav and etrog, then beat the ground with willow (aravah) branches
Practice: music and dancing commemorate a water-drawing ceremony, Simchat Beit HaSho'evah ("Rejoicing of the Place of Water-Drawing"), during Sukkot's intermediate days
Practice: a golden vessel was historically used to collect the water libations poured during this celebration
Practice: a reception is held in the sukkah during this celebration
Holiday: begins on the 15th of Tishrei, five days after Yom Kippur, and lasts seven days (eight outside Israel)
Holiday: immediately followed by Shemini Atzeret and then Simchat Torah
Holiday: the transition into Shemini Atzeret features the rain prayer Geshem, recited while wearing a white robe
Holiday: commemorates the Israelites' forty years wandering the desert after the Exodus, sheltered by God's protective "clouds of glory"
Holiday: one of the Shalosh Regalim (the three biblical pilgrimage festivals), the first celebrated after Rosh Hashanah
Holiday: Samaritans commemorate this holiday by building a trellis and traveling to Mount Gerizim
Holiday: Hallel (Psalms 113-118) is recited during this holiday, as on several others
Holiday: the entire Book of Psalms is read on one day of this holiday, when celebrants greet each other with "pitka tava"
Holiday: Moses established the 15th of the seventh month as this holiday's start date
Holiday: Exodus 34 refers to this holiday as the "Feast of Ingathering"
Holiday: Jeroboam instituted a false/perverted version of this holiday at an altar in Bethel in the northern Kingdom of Israel

Holiday: Ezra revived this holiday's celebration by reading the Law at the Water Gate, and it was the first holiday observed in Jerusalem after Temple sacrifices resumed following the Babylonian Captivity
Holiday: Nehemiah instituted this holiday's observance after completing the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls
Holiday: the prophet Zechariah (ch. 14) warns that Gentiles who refuse to celebrate this holiday in the eschaton will suffer drought and a flesh-rotting plague
Holiday: in the New Testament, Paul left Ephesus to observe this "feast of tabernacles"
Holiday: in the Gospel of John, Jesus proclaims that "rivers of living water" would flow from believers, on this holiday's final day
Holiday: scholar Johannes C. de Moor has posited a connection between this holiday and the Ugaritic New Year
Holiday: once every seven years, the king publicly read the Torah during this festival, in a practice called Hakhel
Holiday: according to 2 Maccabees, the "first Hanukkah" celebrated by the Maccabees was actually a delayed observance of this festival
Holiday: the obligation to bring first fruits (bikkurim) to the Temple, beginning at Shavuot, concluded at this festival
Holiday: also known as Z'man Simchateinu ("Season of Rejoicing")
Holiday: contains the last of the "Days of Judgment" in the Jewish liturgical calendar
